---
title: "Spatial metabolomics workflow using the cherry_tomato_msi example dataset"
author: "Xinyin Deng"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Spatial metabolomics workflow using the cherry_tomato_msi example dataset}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  collapse = TRUE,
  eval=TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  fig.align = "center"
)
```

# Overview
This tutorial demonstrates a minimal spatial metabolomics workflow using the
`cherry_tomato_msi` example dataset included in `ARBO`.

`cherry_tomato_msi` is a toy `MSImagingExperiment` object designed for package examples and tutorials. It is not a real MSI acquisition dataset. The spatial structure was constructed to resemble the cherry tomato, and the m/z features were manually defined for demonstration purposes. Therefore, the m/z values in this dataset should not be given biological or chemical interpretation.

Despite its simplified nature, the dataset is useful for illustrating:

- MSI object inspection
- spectral visualization
- ion image visualization
- dimension reduction
- spatial clustering
- clustering with `ARBO`

For reference tutorials on the Cardinal package, please see [Cardinal vignettes](https://github.com/kuwisdelu/CardinalWorkflows/tree/devel/vignettes)

```{r tomato-image, echo=FALSE, out.width='60%', fig.cap='Reference image used to construct the cherry_tomato_msi toy dataset.'}
knitr::include_graphics(system.file("extdata", "Cherry_tomato.png", package = "ARBO"))
```

# Load packages and example data
```{r Load-MSI-data,echo=TRUE}
# if (!require("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE))
#     install.packages("BiocManager")
# BiocManager::install("Cardinal")
# install.packages("remotes")
# remotes::install_github("DengXinyin/ARBO")

library(Cardinal)
library(ARBO)
data(cherry_tomato_msi)
tomato <- cherry_tomato_msi
tomato
```

The example dataset contains 66 m/z features and 27680 spectra. It is intentionally lightweight so that examples remain fast, stable, and reproducible.

## Spectrum visualization

Inspect the average spectrum across pixels. Below we will plot the mean spectrum and annotate several peaks.

```{r MSI-mean,echo=TRUE,fig.height=6,fig.width=12}
tomato <- summarizeFeatures(tomato, c(Mean="mean"))

Cardinal::plot(
  tomato,
  isPeaks = TRUE,
  annPeaks = 6,
  "Mean", 
  xlab="m/z", ylab="Intensity"
)
```

Because this is a toy dataset with manually defined m/z values, the peak pattern
should be interpreted only as a visualization example.

## Ion images visualization

We can display ion images for all available m/z features.

```{r MSI-TIC,echo=TRUE,fig.height=6,fig.width=8}
tomato <- summarizePixels(tomato, c(TIC="sum"))

Cardinal::image(tomato, "TIC")

image(
  tomato,
  mz = featureData(tomato)$mz,
  smooth = "gaussian",
  enhance = "hist",
  scale = TRUE,
  key = TRUE
)
```

This panel provides a quick view of spatial intensity variation across the toy cherry tomato section. Because the dataset was constructed for demonstration, the resulting ion maps are useful for illustrating MSI image visualization workflows, but they should not be interpreted biologically.

# Spatial clustering with unsupervised methods 
## Principal component analysis (PCA) 
PCA provides a low-dimensional summary of the spectral variation across pixels.
```{r PCA,echo=TRUE}
tomato_pca <- Cardinal::PCA(tomato, ncomp = 2)
tomato_pca
```

We can visualize the PCA components as spatial images.
```{r PCA-plot,echo=TRUE,fig.height=4,fig.width=6}
image(
  tomato_pca,
  smooth = "adaptive",
  enhance = "histogram"
)
```

PCA is useful for identifying dominant global patterns in the dataset, although
in this toy example the components mainly reflect the manually constructed structure in the data.

## Non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) 
NMF is another commonly used decomposition method for MSI data.
```{r NMF,echo=TRUE}
tomato_nmf <- Cardinal::NMF(tomato, ncomp = 2, niter = 30)
tomato_nmf
```

We again visualize the resulting components.
```{r NMF-plot,echo=TRUE,fig.height=4,fig.width=6}
image(
  tomato_nmf,
  smooth = "adaptive",
  enhance = "histogram"
)
```

NMF often produces parts-based spatial patterns, which can be easier to interpret in imaging data.

## Spatial shrunken centroids (SSC)
`Cardinal` provides the `spatialShrunkenCentroids()` method for spatially aware segmentation / classification of MSI data.
```{r spatialShrunkenCentroids,echo=TRUE}
set.seed(2026)
tomato_ssc <- Cardinal::spatialShrunkenCentroids(
  tomato,
  weights = "adaptive",
  r = 2,
  k = 2,
  s = 2
)

tomato_ssc
```

We can display the resulting segmentation map.
```{r spatialShrunkenCentroids-plot,echo=TRUE,fig.height=4,fig.width=6}
image(tomato_ssc, i = 1)
```

This gives a spatially smoothed cluster assignment across the tissue region.

## Clustering with ARBO

Next, we will demonstrate the clustering workflow implemented in `ARBO`.

Because this step depends on an external Python environment, the code is shown but not evaluated automatically in the vignette.

The Python backend was tested with a conda environment containing Python 3.9, `numpy` 1.24.4, and `umap-learn` 0.5.7. 
```{bash, eval=FALSE}
conda create -n dxy_python9 python=3.9 -y
conda activate dxy_python9
conda install -c conda-forge numpy=1.24.4 umap-learn=0.5.7 -y
```
Other compatible versions may also work when properly configured through `reticulate`.

```{r ARBO-clustering,echo=TRUE}
# Example conda environment setup -------------------
res <- ARBO::spatial_clustering_workflow(
  msi_obj = tomato,
  # python_path = "/path/to/conda/env/bin/python", # use your python path
  python_path = "/home/hsinyinteng/miniconda3/envs/dxy_python9/bin/python",
  clustering_method = "kmeans",
  centers = 2L,  # number of clusters
  metric = "cosine",
  n_neighbors = 15L,
  min_dist = 0.1,
  n_components = 2L,
  n_jobs = 1L,
  umap_seed = NULL,
  verbose = TRUE
) 
head(res$cluster_df)

# # kmeans
# res <- spatial_clustering_workflow(
#   msi_obj = tomato,
#   clustering_method = "kmeans",
#   centers = 2L
# )
# 
# # gmm
# res <- spatial_clustering_workflow(
#   msi_obj = tomato,
#   clustering_method = "gmm",
#   centers = 2L
# )
# 
# # fcm
# res <- spatial_clustering_workflow(
#   msi_obj = tomato,
#   clustering_method = "fcm",
#   centers = 2L
# )
# 
# # dbscan
# res <- spatial_clustering_workflow(
#   msi_obj = tomato,
#   clustering_method = "dbscan",
#   eps = 0.3,
#   minPts = 2L
# )

# hclust
# res <- spatial_clustering_workflow(
#   msi_obj = tomato,
#   clustering_method = "hclust",
#   centers = 2L
# )
```

### Plot the clustering result
The output cluster assignments can be visualized using ggplot2.
```{r ARBO-plot,echo=TRUE,fig.height=4,fig.width=6}
library(ggplot2)
cluster_df <- res$cluster_df

ggplot(cluster_df, aes(x, y, color = factor(cluster))) +
  geom_point(size = 0.1) +
  scale_y_reverse() +
  coord_fixed() +
  theme_void() +
  labs(color = "Cluster") +
  guides(
    color = guide_legend(
      override.aes = list(size = 6)
    )
  )
```
This plot provides a simple spatial view of the cluster labels produced by the workflow.

# Screening spatially enriched metabolites (SEMs)

In this example, SEMs refer to metabolites showing strong spatial association with the predefined **Calyx** and **Fruit** regions stored in
`pixelData(tomato)$ROI`.

The screening process uses the existing pixel annotation in `pixelData(tomato)$ROI`, which labels pixels as **Calyx** or **Fruit**.

For each metabolite, `SEMs_screen()` combines statistics from `spatialShrunkenCentroids()` and `colocalized()` to evaluate whether the feature is spatially enriched in one of these regions. The screening criteria include the SSC t-statistic, colocalization correlation (`cor`), and the
abundance and specificity measures `M1` and `M2`. By applying user-defined thresholds to these statistics, the function identifies metabolites with strong region-associated spatial patterns.

```{r SEMs-screen,echo=TRUE}
res_SEMs <- SEMs_screen(
  msi_obj = tomato,
  group_col = "ROI",
  t_statistic_threshold = 20,
  cor_threshold = 0.5,
  M1_threshold = 0.5,
  M2_threshold = 0.5,
  save_table = FALSE,
  file_name = "Spatially_Enriched_Metabolites",
  file_format = "xlsx"
)

dim(res_SEMs$results_df)
dim(res_SEMs$SEMs_df)
table(res_SEMs$SEMs_df$class)

image(tomato,
      mz=sort(res_SEMs$SEMs_df$mz),
      smooth="gaussian",
      enhance="hist",
      scale=TRUE,
      key=FALSE)

```
As a result of the screening process, 55 out of the original 66 features were selected as SEMs. Based on their spatial distribution patterns, 28 of these features were predominantly localized to the Calyx, while the remaining 27 features were associated with the Fruit region.

It is important to note that these statistical results were obtained under idealized conditions, as the **cherry_tomato_msi** dataset was manually generated. When applying this screening approach to real-world MSI data, the corresponding thresholds may need to be adjusted to account for biological variability and technical noise inherent in experimental datasets.

# Spatially enriched metabolites are sufficient to reconstruct MSI spatial architecture

We next asked whether the spatial structure of the dataset could be recovered without using the full set of detected ions. To address this, spatial clustering was performed using only spatially enriched metabolites (SEMs). The resulting clustering pattern still recapitulated the main spatial organization of the toy MSI dataset, indicating that SEMs retain the dominant spatially informative variation. This illustrates that a reduced set of region-associated features can serve as a compact proxy for the full MSI feature space. In practice, such feature reduction can improve interpretability and reduce noise from less informative ions.

```{r,echo=TRUE}
tomato_SEMs_only <- subsetFeatures(
  tomato,
  featureData(tomato)$mz %in% sort(res_SEMs$SEMs_df$mz)
)
tomato_SEMs_only

res <- spatial_clustering_workflow(
  msi_obj = tomato_SEMs_only,
  python_path = "/home/hsinyinteng/miniconda3/envs/dxy_python9/bin/python",
  centers = 2
)
cluster_df <- res$cluster_df

library(ggplot2)
ggplot(cluster_df, aes(x, y, color = factor(cluster))) +
  geom_point(size = 0.1) + 
  scale_y_reverse() +
  coord_fixed() +
  theme_void() +
  guides(color = guide_legend(override.aes = list(size = 6))) +
  labs(color = "Cluster")
```

# Image overlay 

This module provides an image overlay approach for result visualization. It can be used to highlight the distribution of a selected m/z feature or annotation within a region of interest (ROI), while preserving its spatial context in the full MSI dataset.

In the examples below, the total ion current (TIC) image is used as the background layer, and either an ion image or the `cluster` annotation from a
subsetted ROI is overlaid on top. This makes it easier to visualize how a region-specific signal is positioned within the overall sample.

```{r,echo=TRUE}
fruit <- subsetPixels(tomato, pixelData(tomato)$ROI == "Fruit")
fruit

msi_img_overlay(
  msi_data01 = tomato,
  value01 = "TIC",
  color01 = colorRampPalette(c("white", "grey"))(100),
  msi_data02 = tomato,
  value02 = 558.4396,
  color02 = colorRampPalette(c("black", "blue", "cyan", "yellow", "red"))(100),
  show_axes = FALSE
  )

msi_img_overlay(
  msi_data01 = tomato,
  value01 = "TIC",
  color01 = colorRampPalette(c("white", "grey"))(100),
  msi_data02 = fruit,
  value02 = "ROI",
  color02 = c(
    Calyx = "#E64B35",
    Fruit = "#3C5488"
  ),
  show_axes = FALSE
)
```

# Notes on interpretation
The **cherry_tomato_msi** dataset is intentionally lightweight and simplified. It is designed to make package examples fast, stable, and easy to understand.

A few caveats should be kept in mind:

- The dataset contains only 66 m/z features.
- The m/z values are manually defined and have no real biochemical meaning.
- The spatial patterns are designed for demonstration rather than derived from a real MSI experiment.
- Results are appropriate for workflow illustration, but not for biological inference or method benchmarking.

# Acknowledgements
This package is designed to work with `Cardinal` MSI objects, and some demonstration steps in this vignette follow a workflow style similar to the
Cardinal tutorials. 

The included `cherry_tomato_msi` dataset is a manually constructed toy example created for demonstration purposes and is not derived
from the example datasets distributed with Cardinal.

# Session Information
```{r SessionInfo,echo=TRUE}
sessionInfo()
```
